title: The Still After
prompt: Close on a hand—ochre-stained fingers gripping a flint blade. Behind the hand, blurred, a woman's silhouette crouches on a rocky ridge. Falling snow between them. A fire pit glows faint in a shallow cave. Two figures, one known, one not yet. **Narration** Var finishes the blade. Sets it aside. Picks up a cracked hand-axe and examines the fracture line. He heard her breathing twenty minutes ago. The wind carried it. Human lungs are smaller. Shallower breath. Fast when they're afraid. She isn't breathing fast now. That's something. He has no interest in killing anyone. The Ash Council would disagree. They sent him to hold the pass, to turn away the River people. He was supposed to be a wall. He is a toolmaker. He makes things that work. The snow is coming harder. If she stays up there another hour, the rocks will ice over. She'll slide. Break something. Die alone in the cold. He sets the hand-axe down. Reaches into a leather sack. Pulls out a strip of dried meat. Sets it on a flat stone near the fire's edge. "You haven't eaten," he says. Silence. Wind. Snow. He doesn't look up. "I haven't either." He waits. The fire pops. A log shifts. Then—footsteps. Slow. Controlled. Coming down the ridge. He keeps his hands visible. Picks up the whetstone. Resumes his work. She enters the cave's edge. Stops. Spear held

title: The Stone Gate
prompt: A narrow mountain pass choked with rubble. Nara crouches behind a boulder, her spear shaft cold under white-knuckled fingers. Across the gap, a broad-shouldered man in layered furs fits a stone into a low wall, ash-grey curls catching thin light. Smoke threads upward from a fire pit; bone tools lie scattered on hide.
narrative: She tracks the prints for half a day. Wide, five-toed, no arch. Neanderthal. The pass appears between two ridges, blocked by a wall of fitted stones—not a barricade, but a gate. Deliberate gaps offer sightlines. She counts his tools from cover. Twelve. Maybe fourteen. He works with his back to her, hands sure and patient. Unarmed. Her knuckles strain against the ash-wood shaft. An ambush could lurk in the rocks. Or he could be alone, building something quiet. He places a stone, adjusts it, steps back. A single nod at his own work. The wind carries woodsmoke and ochre dust. For twenty breaths, neither moves.
narration: I follow the tracks for half a day. Wide prints, five toes, no arch. Neanderthal. The pass emerges between two ridges, choked with rubble. A wall of fitted stones blocks the narrowest point. Not a barricade—a gate. I count his tools from my boulder. Twelve. Maybe fourteen. He works with his back to me, fingers patient on the rock. Unarmed. My knuckles are white around the spear shaft. An ambush could be set in these crags. Or he could be alone, building something useful. He places a stone, adjusts it, steps back. Nods once to himself. Wind carries smoke and ochre dust. I wait. He doesn’t know I’m here. Twenty minutes pass, and neither of us moves.

title: The Failed Exchange
prompt: Dusk in a narrow mountain pass. Nara crouches, placing a bundle of silvery fish and smooth river stones on a flat granite slab. Across the gap, Var holds up a polished bone awl, its tip catching the last light. Distant smoke smudges the twin ridgelines.
narrative: The air bites with the coming night. Nara places the bundle, the fish scales glinting like frozen whispers. She retreats three measured steps, her hand brushing the blue stones at her temple. From the treeline, Var lifts the awl. He turns it slowly, the bone worn smooth by his palm. He takes a step. A horn bellows from the ridge—a low, vibrating warning. Var freezes, his shoulders locking. His gaze darts upward, then back to her. The awl clatters to the frost-bitten ground. He steps back, melting into the shadows. Above, pebbles cascade down the slope. Nara snatches the bundle and flees, the pass closing around her like a fist. Var retrieves his tool, eyes fixed on the watching ridge. Nine breaths of possibility, evaporated into stone-cold air.
narration: The scent of brine and frost fills the pass. Nara sets the trade-gift down, the fish a pale smear on dark rock. She counts her steps back—three, a human measure of caution. Var emerges, the bone awl held high, a question of worth in his broad hand. He steps forward. The horn splits the silence. It isn't a hunter's call. It's a decree. Var flinches, the tool dropping from his fingers. He looks at her, then at the unseen watchers on the ridge. A whole negotiation in that glance, broken before a word. Nara runs, the bundle a cold weight in her arms. The pass contracts behind her. Var stays. He picks up the awl, its edge still sharp, his gaze still on the heights where authority festers. The silence after is louder than the horn.

title: The Avalanche
prompt: The mountain pass crumbles, unleashing a roaring white wave that buries the disputed goods. Nara clings to a granite shelf, wind whipping her braids. Below, Var hunchs over a child, his broad back braced for the impact. The valley is filled with a crushing, sudden silence.
narrative: The mountain's groan becomes a shriek. Granite vibrates under Nara’s palms. White fury funnels through the choke point, a liquid wall devouring everything. Var’s head snaps up. His tool-pendant skates across his chest. There is no time. He wraps his arms around the nearest human child, tucking her small form against his ribs, and bows his head to the storm. The snow hits his back like a solid fist. It drives him forward, face into the slope, a living anchor. The roar ends. A muffled cough rises from the drift. Nara peers over the ledge. Only Var’s ash-grey curls are visible, a dark reef in the white. The child whimpers beneath him, shielded by stone and sinew. Three humans. Two Neanderthals. Strangers, gasping in the powder-choked air. The pass is a solid wall. Both paths home are entombed. Var’s arm emerges. His ochre-stained fingers claw weakly at the packed snow. Nara descends, her boots sinking deep. She drops to her knees beside him. Her fingers join his in the cold work. No words pass between them. Only the rhythm of breath and digging.
narration: The mountain's groan becomes a shriek. Granite vibrates under Nara’s palms. White fury funnels through the choke point, a liquid wall devouring everything. Var’s head snaps up. His tool-pendant skates across his chest. There is no time. He wraps his arms around the nearest human child, tucking her small form against his ribs, and bows his head to the storm. The snow hits his back like a solid fist. It drives him forward, face into the slope, a living anchor. The roar ends. A muffled cough rises from the drift. Nara peers over the ledge. Only Var’s ash-grey curls are visible, a dark reef in the white. The child whimpers beneath him, shielded by stone and sinew. Three humans. Two Neanderthals. Strangers, gasping in the powder-choked air. The pass is a solid wall. Both paths home are entombed. Var’s arm emerges. His ochre-stained fingers claw weakly at the packed snow. Nara descends, her boots sinking deep. She drops to her knees beside him. Her fingers join his in the cold work. No words pass between them. Only the rhythm of breath and digging.

title: A Shared Fire
prompt: Snow-filled valley at dusk. Two figures crouch on opposite sides of a small fire built in the lee of a rockfall. Steam rises from their breath in the blue air. Ice catches the firelight. Their shadows overlap on the stone between them.
narration: The fire is small. It has to be. They burned through half the dry moss getting it started. Nara feeds it one sliver of willow at a time. Var shapes a reflector from flat stone, the surface already blackened. It throws a thin sheet of heat back toward the hollow where they sit. The silence is thick. The avalanche killed seven. Three from Nara’s scouting party. Four from Var’s crew. They pulled bodies until their fingers burned, then stopped when the snow began to harden like ice over the remains. Var strips bark from a branch. He carves without looking. The shape emerges—a small, four-legged figure. A child’s toy. His hands know the motion. His gaze stays on the fire. Nara watches the carving take form. She doesn’t ask who it’s for. The cold deepens. The fire gutters. Var shifts closer and feeds it a piece of dried root. The smell is sweet and acrid. Smoke stings her eyes. “You go south after this,” he tells her. His voice is low. “Back to your river people.” Nara presses her palms toward the flames. The skin is cracked and white at the knuckles. “The Ash Council sent me to find your pass,” she says. “They wanted to know if it could be blocked.” Var stops carving. The bone pendant at his chest catches the firelight. He turns the small animal over in his fingers. “And?” “I found it. Three days ago. Before the snow.” She looks at him. “It can be blocked. A single rockfall closes it for the season.” He nods. The carving drops into the snow beside his knee. “The Council will send someone else if you don’t report,” he says. “They will.” The fire pops. An ember dies against the reflector. Var adjusts it without haste. For a long time, they listen to the wind scour the valley. Somewhere distant, ice shifts against ice. The sound is deep and hollow. Var pulls the bone pendant over his head. He holds it in his open palm. The surface is worn smooth from years of touch. “My father carved this,” he tells her. “He died keeping a pass open. Not this one. One to the east.” His fingers close around it. “The Council on my side wanted it closed. He disagreed.” Nara watches his hand. “We are the same problem,” she says. He almost smiles. It doesn’t reach his eyes. The fire dies to coals. They don’t rebuild it. Instead, Var repositions the reflector stones to trap what warmth remains. Nara unlaces her reed cloak and spreads half of it across the space between them. He takes the edge without comment. She lies on her side. He sits upright, his back against the rock. “If I go back with nothing,” she says, “they’ll send soldiers to the pass.” “If I go back with nothing,” he says, “they’ll send me to guard it.” Silence. Then: “There is a cave system past the northern ridge,” she says. “Warm. Defensible. Room for thirty, maybe more.” He turns his head. The firelight catches his profile—the broad jaw, the ash-grey curls matted with old snow. “Your people’s territory.” “My people’s old territory. They left it three winters ago. The game moved on.” She pauses. “My people don’t go back because the Ash Council declared the northern ridge forbidden. Said the ground was cursed.” “And you believe that?” “The ground isn’t cursed. The Council is.” Var is quiet. The wind shifts, carrying new smells—wet stone, old ash, something faintly sweet like rot

title: The Broken Seal
prompt: Firelight illuminates a cave confrontation. A broken bone seal lies between Nara, gripping a knife, and Var, standing protectively. Ash Council figures recede into shadow. Snow falls through cracks overhead.
narrative: The seal hit stone. Bone split along the stag's spine with a dry crack. Nara's fingers found her knife, blue beads clicking against the bone handle. Var stepped between the council and the pile of sleeping children, his broad shoulders blotting out the fire. Ash from his forge work dusted his knuckles. The lead elder's voice was flat: "The valley belongs to separate peoples." She gestured at the fractured roof where snow drifted down like ash. Then they were gone, leaving perfect footprints in the dust. The fire popped. A child coughed in the furs. Var knelt, fitting the two halves of the seal together. The break was clean, held for a breath. He let them clatter apart. Nara counted: fourteen Neanderthals, nine humans, three children old enough to remember. The snow melted on Var's fire stones, steam rising with the scent of wet stone and char. He stood and took a heavy rock to the largest crack. Not a decision. Just the next thing to do.
narration: The snap of bone echoed as the elder shattered the seal—stag and hawk separated forever. Nara’s knife was in her hand before the pieces hit the ground, its bone handle cold against her palm. Var moved without a word, a shield of shoulder and ash-dusted knuckles before the children. His silence was louder than the elder’s decree about separate peoples. She pointed to the broken roof as proof; snow was already blanketing their shared fire pit. Then they left, six straight lines in the ash. Quiet rushed back in, broken only by a child’s cough. Var picked up the halves. For a second, the carved lines matched. Then he let them drop. Twenty-three adults, three children. The roof snow hissed on his fire stones, filling the air with wet stone and smoke. He hefted a rock and started to patch the wall. Not a choice. Just survival.

title: The Ash-Coded Map
prompt: Nara and Var kneel in skeletal watchtower ruins. Wind whistles through shattered stone. Between them, a cured hide scrap bears deliberate claw-marks—a false boundary. Their stone tools lie discarded in the dust.
narrative: The wind bit through Nara’s cloak, carrying grit and the scent of old fires. Her finger followed the claw-lines. Too even. A pattern pressed by a steady hand, not torn by a hungry beast. Var lifted the bone-handled stylus from the debris. Its tip was gleaming, recently honed. He turned it, showing her the deeply burned sigil of the Ash Council on its base. Nara’s breath fogged the frigid air. She looked from the false frontier line to the charcoal smudge marking the true pass beneath it—a lie meant to ignite a war. Var set the stylus down with a soft click. His silence was heavier than stone. He traced a line on the hide from the false boundary to the sheer cliff face above the pass, then drew a finger across his own throat. Nara’s nod was barely a movement. The choice was made. The map wasn't a guide; it was a fuse.
narration: The wind bit through Nara’s cloak, carrying grit and the scent of old fires. Her finger followed the claw-lines. Too even. A pattern pressed by a steady hand, not torn by a hungry beast. Var lifted the bone-handled stylus from the debris. Its tip was gleaming, recently honed. He turned it, showing her the deeply burned sigil of the Ash Council on its base. Nara’s breath fogged the frigid air. She looked from the false frontier line to the charcoal smudge marking the true pass beneath it—a lie meant to ignite a war. Var set the stylus down with a soft click. His silence was heavier than stone. He traced a line on the hide from the false boundary to the sheer cliff face above the pass, then drew a finger across his own throat. Nara’s nod was barely a movement. The choice was made. The map wasn't a guide; it was a fuse.

title: The Ash Vote
prompt: Two figures press against a frost-rimed fallen pine in a narrow valley. Breath plumes in the blue dark. Distant campfire smoke coils upward. Across the gap, a semicircle of hooded figures surrounds an elder whose hands are raised, palms flat, ash white against night. The elder’s gesture is sharp. One by one, the hooded forms lower their heads in assent.
narration: Twelve hooded shapes. Seven he had sharpened flint for. Var’s gaze tracks each bowed head. The elder’s hand chops down, final as a cleaving stone. Nara’s fingers whiten on her spear shaft; the wood groans under her grip. Beside her, Var’s jaw sets like granite. The bone pendant at his throat presses inward, a cold point of accusation. The smoke drifting toward them carries the greasy scent of rendered fat—human fat—from the feast meant to seal their unity. Nara’s hand finds his arm, a brief pressure through woven reed. He does not recoil. She withdraws. The council’s murmurs are lost to the wind. They turn together, downhill. One path of trampled snow, leading away from coals, toward the dark, narrowing throat of the pass. Their prints fill instantly with silence.
